docs.title: Spatial Data Science & AI
docs.text:
I am a PhD Researcher and Geosensing Engineer specializing in Spatial Data Science, Remote Sensing, and Machine Learning.

My work bridges physical geoprocesses, high-performance computing (HPC), and deep learning. I develop automated pipeline and modeling solutions to process high-resolution Earth observations (optical, radar/SAR, LiDAR, and hyperspectral), and fuse them with land surface models to solve complex hydrological, glacial, and permafrost challenges.

docs.title: Core Specializations
docs.text:
• Geospatial & Remote Sensing

    • Active and passive sensor analysis (LiDAR, optical, SAR, hyperspectral).
    • Satellite data fusion (Landsat, Sentinel, SWOT, VIIRS, MODIS, ICESat-2).
    • Large-scale terrain modeling and land surface simulation.

  • AI & Deep Learning

    • Computer Vision & Super-Resolution models for river and water classification.
    • Transformer models and custom geospatial embeddings (e.g., Clay, Prithvi).
    • State estimation and data assimilation (e.g., Kalman filtering).

  • High-Performance Computing

    • Scale-out distributed workflow systems utilizing SLURM, MPI, and Dask.
    • Multi-GPU/Distributed Data Parallel (DDP) deep learning training.
    • Cloud integration (AWS, serverless Lambda execution).

docs.title: 🌊 NASA Regional Hydrology & SWOT Mission
docs.text: Developed machine learning algorithms and spatial pipelines to ingest and process SWOT (Surface Water and Ocean Topography) observations. Created interactive visualization dashboards (swotvis.cuahsi.io) to monitor surface water elevation and river width dynamics worldwide in support of international hydrology sciences.

docs.title: ❄️ Daily Snow Water Equivalent (SWE) of North America
docs.text: Architected a large-scale data fusion pipeline on high-performance computers. We integrated NASA MODIS and VIIRS satellite snow observations with land surface simulation outputs, generating a pristine 20-year daily continential-scale Snow Water Equivalent grid at 0.01° high resolution.

docs.title: 🏔️ Glacier Velocity and Sentinel-2 Corrections
docs.text: Co-developed empirical correction workflows to eliminate systematic orthorectification offsets from Sentinel-2 velocity fields. Applied these pipelines to map glacial ice stream dynamics across the outlets and margins of Greenland.

docs.title: 🧪 Automated Laser Elevation Processing
docs.text: Coded open-source Python toolsets for ICESat-2 altimetry analysis to calibrate and correct systematic vertical biases in continental-scale ArcticDEM, REMA DEM, and WorldDEM terrain models.

docs.title: Technical Paradigms Focus
docs.text: I specialize in leveraging advanced deep-learning frameworks tailored specifically for spatio-temporal datasets: 1. Parameter Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT): Adapting massive foundational vision and remote-sensing models (e.g., Clay, Prithvi) on localized target variables with low weight budgets. 2. Scalable Pipelines: Integrating custom models on clusters running under SLURM or Dask schedulers with parallel multi-node resources.

docs.title: Scaling Deep Learning: Hardware Acceleration, Memory Management, and Distributed (DDP) Architectures
1. System-Level Topology
docs.text:
Modern deep learning training is dominated by large tensor operations such as matrix multiplication, convolution, and attention. GPUs are designed for high-throughput parallel computation, which makes them well suited for neural network training.

At a high level:

CPU: good at general-purpose control logic and sequential work
GPU: good at doing many similar numerical operations in parallel

Deep learning models repeatedly apply the same mathematical operations to large batches of data. This maps naturally to GPU hardware.

flowchart LR
    A[Training Data] --> B[Model Forward Pass]
    B --> C[Loss Computation]
    C --> D[Backward Pass / Gradients]
    D --> E[Optimizer Step]
    E --> B

docs.title: 2. Why GPUs Are Useful for AI
docs.text:
GPUs are useful for AI because neural networks are mostly built from dense linear algebra.

Common GPU-accelerated operations include:

  • matrix multiplication
  • convolution
  • attention
  • normalization
  • activation functions
  • tensor reshaping and reductions

The most important operation in many models is matrix multiplication:

Y = XW

where:

  • X is input data
  • W is model weights
  • Y is output activations

These operations can be split into many small parallel computations, which GPUs handle efficiently.

docs.title: 3. CPU vs GPU Mental Model
docs.text:
A useful simplified view:

CPU = few powerful workers
GPU = many simpler workers

CPUs are strong for:

  • operating system tasks
  • job orchestration
  • data loading
  • preprocessing
  • control flow

GPUs are strong for:

  • tensor math
  • training neural networks
  • large-batch inference
  • matrix multiplication

In deep learning training, the CPU often feeds data to the GPU, while the GPU performs the heavy numerical computation.

flowchart LR
    A[Disk / Dataset] --> B[CPU DataLoader]
    B --> C[Preprocessing]
    C --> D[GPU Tensor Computation]
    D --> E[Loss / Gradients]

docs.title: 4. Single-GPU Training
docs.text:
Single-GPU training is the simplest case.

A typical PyTorch workflow looks like:

load data → move batch to GPU → forward pass → loss → backward pass → optimizer step

Conceptually:

flowchart TD
    A[Batch of Data] --> B[Move to GPU]
    B --> C[Model Forward]
    C --> D[Loss]
    D --> E[Backward / Gradients]
    E --> F[Optimizer Step]

In PyTorch, this usually means:

model = model.to("cuda")
inputs = inputs.to("cuda")
outputs = model(inputs)
loss = criterion(outputs, targets)
loss.backward()
optimizer.step()

Single-GPU training is easy to reason about, but it is limited by:

  • GPU memory
  • GPU compute capacity
  • data loading speed
  • training time

docs.title: 5. When One GPU Is Not Enough
docs.text:
Multiple GPUs may be needed when:

  1. The model is too large for one GPU.
  2. The batch size is too large for one GPU.
  3. Training time on one GPU is too slow.
  4. The dataset is very large.
  5. The model is a large Transformer or other foundation model.

There are different ways to use multiple GPUs depending on the bottleneck.

docs.title: 6. Types of Multi-GPU Parallelism
docs.text:
The major categories are:

  1. Data parallelism
  2. Model parallelism
  3. Tensor parallelism
  4. Pipeline parallelism
  5. Hybrid parallelism
flowchart TD
    A[Multi-GPU Training] --> B[Data Parallelism]
    A --> C[Model / Tensor Parallelism]
    A --> D[Pipeline Parallelism]
    A --> E[Hybrid Parallelism]

For many users and many research workflows, data parallelism with PyTorch DistributedDataParallel (DDP) is the most common starting point.

docs.title: 7. Data Parallelism
docs.text:
Data parallelism means:

same model on each GPU
different data on each GPU
synchronize gradients after backward pass

Example with four GPUs:

GPU0: model copy + batch A
GPU1: model copy + batch B
GPU2: model copy + batch C
GPU3: model copy + batch D

flowchart LR
    A[Dataset Batch] --> B[Split Batch]
    B --> C[GPU0: batch A]
    B --> D[GPU1: batch B]
    B --> E[GPU2: batch C]
    B --> F[GPU3: batch D]

    C --> G[Gradients]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Average Gradients]
    H --> I[Update All Model Copies]

Each GPU computes gradients based on its own mini-batch. Then gradients are averaged so that all model copies remain synchronized.

docs.title: 8. Effective Batch Size
docs.text:
In data parallel training, the effective batch size increases with the number of GPUs.

effective_batch_size = batch_size_per_gpu × number_of_gpus

Example:

batch_size_per_gpu = 32
number_of_gpus = 4

effective_batch_size = 32 × 4 = 128

This matters because changing effective batch size can affect optimization behavior. Sometimes the learning rate or training schedule needs adjustment when scaling to many GPUs.

docs.title: 9. PyTorch DataParallel vs DistributedDataParallel
docs.text:
PyTorch has historically offered two common approaches:

  1. torch.nn.DataParallel
  2. torch.nn.parallel.DistributedDataParallel

DataParallel is simpler but generally not recommended for modern multi-GPU training. It uses one Python process and splits work across GPUs, often creating a bottleneck on the primary GPU.

DistributedDataParallel (DDP) is the recommended approach. It uses one process per GPU and performs efficient gradient synchronization.

DataParallel:
1 process → multiple GPUs

DistributedDataParallel:
multiple processes → one GPU per process

flowchart TD
    A[Multi-GPU PyTorch] --> B[DataParallel]
    A --> C[DistributedDataParallel]
    B --> B1[Single process; easier but slower]
    C --> C1[One process per GPU; recommended]

docs.title: 10. DDP Core Mental Model
docs.text:
The most useful mental model for DDP is:

Each GPU has one process.
Each process has one model copy.
Each process receives different data.
Gradients are synchronized every training step.

With four GPUs:

Process 0 → GPU0 → model copy → data shard A
Process 1 → GPU1 → model copy → data shard B
Process 2 → GPU2 → model copy → data shard C
Process 3 → GPU3 → model copy → data shard D

flowchart LR
    P0[Process 0] --> G0[GPU 0]
    P1[Process 1] --> G1[GPU 1]
    P2[Process 2] --> G2[GPU 2]
    P3[Process 3] --> G3[GPU 3]

    G0 --> S[Gradient Synchronization]
    G1 --> S
    G2 --> S
    G3 --> S

docs.title: 11. What torchrun Does
docs.text:
For DDP, you usually do not launch the script with plain python train.py when using multiple GPUs.

Instead, you use:

torchrun --nproc_per_node=4 train.py

This launches four Python processes on the node.

torchrun sets environment variables such as:

LOCAL_RANK
RANK
WORLD_SIZE

Meaning:

  • LOCAL_RANK: process index on the local node, often maps to local GPU ID
  • RANK: global process index across all processes
  • WORLD_SIZE: total number of processes

For one node with four GPUs:

WORLD_SIZE = 4
RANK = 0, 1, 2, 3
LOCAL_RANK = 0, 1, 2, 3

docs.title: 12. Same Node Multi-GPU Training
docs.text:
When all GPUs are on the same physical node, communication happens through local GPU interconnects such as PCIe or NVLink.

Example:

torchrun --nproc_per_node=4 train.py

A common Slurm job request might look like:

#SBATCH --nodes=1
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:4
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=8

Inside the job script:

torchrun --nproc_per_node=4 train.py

Same-node multi-GPU is generally easier and faster than multi-node training because communication stays within one machine.

docs.title: 13. Multi-Node Multi-GPU Training
docs.text:
Multi-node training extends the same DDP idea across multiple machines.

Example:

4 nodes × 4 GPUs per node = 16 GPUs total

The conceptual model remains:

one process per GPU
one model copy per process
different data per process
gradient synchronization across all processes

flowchart TD
    subgraph Node1
        A1[GPU0/P0]
        A2[GPU1/P1]
        A3[GPU2/P2]
        A4[GPU3/P3]
    end

    subgraph Node2
        B1[GPU0/P4]
        B2[GPU1/P5]
        B3[GPU2/P6]
        B4[GPU3/P7]
    end

    A1 --> S[Global Gradient Sync]
    A2 --> S
    A3 --> S
    A4 --> S
    B1 --> S
    B2 --> S
    B3 --> S
    B4 --> S

The main difference is that synchronization now crosses the network. This makes communication performance much more important.

docs.title: 14. Gradient Synchronization
docs.text:
In DDP, synchronization happens during the backward pass.

Each GPU computes gradients from its own data. Then DDP averages gradients across all processes.

Conceptually:

final_gradient = average(gradient_GPU0, gradient_GPU1, gradient_GPU2, gradient_GPU3)

Then each process applies the same optimizer update.

This keeps all model copies identical.

Important point:

Gradient synchronization happens every batch / step, not once per epoch.

sequenceDiagram
    participant G0 as GPU0
    participant G1 as GPU1
    participant G2 as GPU2
    participant G3 as GPU3

    G0->>G0: Forward + Backward
    G1->>G1: Forward + Backward
    G2->>G2: Forward + Backward
    G3->>G3: Forward + Backward

    G0->>G1: AllReduce gradients
    G1->>G2: AllReduce gradients
    G2->>G3: AllReduce gradients
    G3->>G0: AllReduce gradients

    G0->>G0: Optimizer step
    G1->>G1: Optimizer step
    G2->>G2: Optimizer step
    G3->>G3: Optimizer step

docs.title: 15. NCCL
docs.text:
NCCL stands for NVIDIA Collective Communications Library.

It is a communication backend optimized for GPU-to-GPU communication.

PyTorch DDP commonly uses NCCL for GPU training:

dist.init_process_group("nccl")

NCCL handles collective operations such as:

  • AllReduce
  • Broadcast
  • ReduceScatter
  • AllGather

For DDP, the most important concept is AllReduce, which is used to combine gradients across GPUs.

docs.title: 16. AllReduce Intuition
docs.text:
AllReduce is a collective operation where all processes contribute values and all receive the combined result.

For gradient averaging:

Each GPU contributes its gradients.
All GPUs receive the averaged gradients.

This avoids sending everything to one central GPU and helps communication scale better.

flowchart LR
    A[GPU0 Gradient] --> E[AllReduce]
    B[GPU1 Gradient] --> E
    C[GPU2 Gradient] --> E
    D[GPU3 Gradient] --> E
    E --> F[Average Gradient on GPU0]
    E --> G[Average Gradient on GPU1]
    E --> H[Average Gradient on GPU2]
    E --> I[Average Gradient on GPU3]

docs.title: 17. DistributedSampler
docs.text:
In DDP, each process should see a different slice of the dataset.

Without this, every GPU might train on the same examples, wasting computation.

PyTorch provides DistributedSampler:

from torch.utils.data.distributed import DistributedSampler

sampler = DistributedSampler(dataset)
loader = DataLoader(dataset, sampler=sampler)

At the start of each epoch, it is common to call:

sampler.set_epoch(epoch)

This ensures proper shuffling across epochs.

docs.title: 18. Minimal DDP Code Pattern
docs.text:
A simplified DDP structure:

import os
import torch
import torch.distributed as dist
from torch.nn.parallel import DistributedDataParallel as DDP


def setup_distributed():
    distributed = "RANK" in os.environ

    if distributed:
        dist.init_process_group("nccl")
        local_rank = int(os.environ["LOCAL_RANK"])
        torch.cuda.set_device(local_rank)
        device = torch.device("cuda", local_rank)
    else:
        local_rank = 0
        device = torch.device("cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu")

    return distributed, local_rank, device


distributed, local_rank, device = setup_distributed()

model = MyModel().to(device)

if distributed:
    model = DDP(model, device_ids=[local_rank])

This pattern allows the same script to run in single-GPU or multi-GPU mode.

docs.title: 19. Single GPU as a Special Case
docs.text:
A robust training script can treat single-GPU training as a special case of distributed training.

Typical execution modes:

python train.py

for single GPU, or:

torchrun --nproc_per_node=4 train.py

for four GPUs.

The code can detect whether distributed variables exist and adapt automatically.

This is useful because the same core training script can scale from a local workstation to an HPC node.

docs.title: 20. Slurm and GPU Allocation
docs.text:
On HPC systems, Slurm allocates GPUs as scheduled resources.

A job may request GPUs like this:

#SBATCH --gres=gpu:1

or:

#SBATCH --gres=gpu:4

Slurm controls which GPUs are visible to the job using environment variables such as:

CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES

If a node has four GPUs and a job requests two, Slurm may expose only two GPUs to that job.

Inside the job, PyTorch sees those assigned GPUs as logical device IDs starting from 0.

docs.title: 21. Node Sharing and GPU Allocation
docs.text:
If a node has multiple GPUs, Slurm may allow multiple users to share the node as long as resources do not conflict.

Example:

Node has 4 GPUs
User A requests 2 GPUs
User B requests 1 GPU

Both jobs may run on the same node if enough CPU, memory, and GPU resources remain.

If a node has only one GPU and two users each request one GPU, both GPU jobs should not run at the same time on that node. One job should wait until the GPU is free.

A GPU job and a CPU-only job can often share the same node if the cluster policy allows it, because only one job needs the GPU.

docs.title: 22. Example Slurm Script: Single GPU
docs.text:
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=single_gpu_train
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:1
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=8
#SBATCH --time=02:00:00
#SBATCH --output=single_gpu_%j.out

module load cuda
conda activate myenv

python train.py

This runs one Python process using one GPU.

docs.title: 23. Example Slurm Script: Single Node, Multi-GPU DDP
docs.text:
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=ddp_single_node
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:4
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=8
#SBATCH --time=02:00:00
#SBATCH --output=ddp_%j.out

module load cuda
conda activate myenv

torchrun --nproc_per_node=4 train.py

This launches four processes, one per GPU.

docs.title: 24. Example Slurm Script: Multi-Node DDP Concept
docs.text:
A multi-node DDP script requires coordination between nodes. The exact script depends on cluster configuration, but conceptually it needs:

  • number of nodes
  • processes per node
  • master address
  • master port
  • node rank

Example structure:

#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=ddp_multi_node
#SBATCH --nodes=2
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:4
#SBATCH --ntasks-per-node=1
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=16
#SBATCH --time=04:00:00
#SBATCH --output=ddp_multinode_%j.out

module load cuda
conda activate myenv

MASTER_ADDR=$(scontrol show hostnames $SLURM_JOB_NODELIST | head -n 1)
MASTER_PORT=29500
NNODES=$SLURM_JOB_NUM_NODES
NPROC_PER_NODE=4
NODE_RANK=$SLURM_NODEID

torchrun \
  --nnodes=$NNODES \
  --nproc_per_node=$NPROC_PER_NODE \
  --node_rank=$NODE_RANK \
  --master_addr=$MASTER_ADDR \
  --master_port=$MASTER_PORT \
  train.py

Some clusters prefer launching with srun; others use torchrun directly inside the allocation. The best approach depends on site policy.

docs.title: 25. Failure Behavior in DDP
docs.text:
Standard DDP is synchronous and not fault tolerant by default.

This means:

slow GPU → all GPUs wait
failed GPU/process → whole job usually fails

DDP does not automatically remove a bad GPU or redistribute its data to other GPUs.

The usual mitigation is checkpointing.

docs.title: 26. Checkpointing
docs.text:
Checkpointing periodically saves training state so that a failed job can restart from the last saved point.

A useful checkpoint may include:

  • model weights
  • optimizer state
  • scheduler state
  • epoch number
  • random number generator states

In DDP, usually only rank 0 saves the checkpoint to avoid multiple processes writing the same file.

Example pattern:

if not distributed or dist.get_rank() == 0:
    torch.save(checkpoint, "checkpoint.pt")

When using DDP, the underlying model may be accessed as:

model.module

because DDP wraps the original model.

docs.title: 27. Straggler Problem
docs.text:
Because DDP is synchronous, all processes must reach synchronization points together.

If one GPU is slow, all other GPUs wait.

Causes of stragglers include:

  • uneven data loading
  • slow storage access
  • overloaded CPU
  • hardware differences
  • network issues
  • thermal throttling
sequenceDiagram
    participant G0 as Fast GPU
    participant G1 as Fast GPU
    participant G2 as Slow GPU

    G0->>G0: Finish backward early
    G1->>G1: Finish backward early
    G2->>G2: Still computing
    G0->>G0: Wait
    G1->>G1: Wait
    G2->>G2: Finish backward
    G0->>G2: Synchronize gradients
    G1->>G2: Synchronize gradients

docs.title: 28. Common Bottlenecks
docs.text: Multi-GPU training can be limited by several bottlenecks.

docs.title: GPU compute bottleneck
docs.text: The model is using the GPU heavily and training speed is limited by GPU math throughput.

docs.title: GPU memory bottleneck
docs.text: The model or batch size is too large for GPU memory.

docs.title: Data loading bottleneck
docs.text: The GPU waits for the CPU or storage system to prepare batches.

docs.title: Communication bottleneck
docs.text: GPUs spend too much time synchronizing gradients.

docs.title: Network bottleneck
docs.text: In multi-node training, inter-node communication may limit scaling.

docs.title: Only one GPU is being used
docs.text:
Likely causes:

  • launched with python train.py instead of torchrun
  • model not wrapped in DDP
  • code hardcoded to one GPU

docs.title: Multi-GPU job is slower than expected
docs.text:
Likely causes:

  • data loading bottleneck
  • too small batch size per GPU
  • excessive communication
  • slow storage
  • imbalance across processes

docs.title: DDP job hangs
docs.text:
Likely causes:

  • one process crashed silently
  • mismatch in number of processes
  • all processes did not call the same collective operation
  • networking or NCCL issue
  • different code path on different ranks

docs.title: Out of memory
docs.text:
Likely fixes:

  • reduce batch size
  • use mixed precision
  • use gradient checkpointing
  • use model/tensor parallelism
  • reduce sequence length

docs.title: 30. Relationship to OpenMP, MPI, Dask, and Joblib
docs.text:
Traditional HPC often uses MPI and OpenMP.

OpenMP:

shared-memory threading within a node

MPI:

process-based distributed communication across nodes

PyTorch DDP is conceptually closer to MPI because it uses multiple processes and collective communication. However, users usually do not write MPI code directly.

Dask and joblib are higher-level Python tools for parallelism.

A useful mapping:

joblib → high-level local parallelism
dask → high-level distributed task parallelism
DDP → distributed synchronous deep learning training
MPI → lower-level distributed message passing
OpenMP → lower-level shared-memory threading

docs.title: 31. Training vs Inference on GPUs
docs.text:
Training requires:

  • forward pass
  • loss computation
  • backward pass
  • gradient synchronization
  • optimizer update

Inference usually requires:

  • forward pass only

Training is typically much more memory-intensive because it must store activations for backpropagation.

For decoder-only Transformers, inference generates one token at a time, often using a KV cache to avoid recomputing previous attention keys and values.

docs.title: 32. Large Transformer Training and GPUs
docs.text:
Large Transformer training may require more than data parallelism.

Reasons:

  • model too large for one GPU
  • optimizer states too large
  • activation memory too large
  • sequence length too long

Additional strategies include:

  • mixed precision training
  • gradient checkpointing
  • tensor parallelism
  • pipeline parallelism
  • ZeRO optimizer sharding
  • fully sharded data parallelism

These techniques reduce memory pressure and/or distribute model components across GPUs.

docs.title: 33. Model Parallelism
docs.text:
Model parallelism splits the model across GPUs.

Example:

GPU0 → first layers
GPU1 → later layers

This is useful when the model does not fit on one GPU.

flowchart LR
    A[Input] --> B[Layers 1-12 on GPU0]
    B --> C[Layers 13-24 on GPU1]
    C --> D[Output]

The downside is that GPUs may wait on each other unless carefully pipelined.

docs.title: 34. Tensor Parallelism
docs.text:
Tensor parallelism splits individual matrix operations across GPUs.

Instead of putting different layers on different GPUs, a single large layer may be partitioned.

Example:

Large weight matrix split across multiple GPUs

This is common in very large language model training.

docs.title: 35. Pipeline Parallelism
docs.text:
Pipeline parallelism splits model layers into stages and sends micro-batches through them.

flowchart LR
    A[Microbatch 1] --> B[Stage 1 / GPU0]
    B --> C[Stage 2 / GPU1]
    C --> D[Stage 3 / GPU2]
    D --> E[Stage 4 / GPU3]

It improves utilization compared with simple layer splitting, but introduces scheduling complexity and pipeline bubbles.

docs.title: 36. Hybrid Parallelism
docs.text:
Large-scale training often combines multiple methods:

data parallelism + tensor parallelism + pipeline parallelism + optimizer sharding

For example:

  • data parallelism across groups of nodes
  • tensor parallelism within a node
  • pipeline parallelism across layers
  • optimizer states sharded across processes

This is common for very large Transformer models.

docs.title: Single GPU
docs.text: one process → one GPU → one model → one batch at a time

docs.title: Single Node Multi-GPU DDP
docs.text: N processes → N GPUs → N model copies → different data → gradient averaging

docs.title: Multi-Node DDP
docs.text: same as above, but processes are spread across nodes and synchronize over the network

docs.title: Data Parallelism
docs.text: split data, replicate model

docs.title: Model Parallelism
docs.text: split model, coordinate computation

docs.title: Pipeline Parallelism
docs.text: split model into stages, stream micro-batches

docs.title: 38. Key Terms Glossary
docs.text:
GPU: Graphics Processing Unit; accelerates parallel tensor computation.

CUDA: NVIDIA programming platform for GPU computing.

CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES: Environment variable controlling which GPUs are visible to a process.

DDP: DistributedDataParallel; PyTorch's recommended multi-GPU training approach.

Rank: Global ID of a distributed process.

Local rank: Process ID within a node, usually used to select the local GPU.

World size: Total number of distributed processes.

NCCL: NVIDIA communication library used for GPU collectives.

AllReduce: Collective operation that combines values across processes and returns the result to all processes.

DistributedSampler: PyTorch sampler that partitions data across DDP processes.

Effective batch size: Batch size per GPU multiplied by number of GPUs.

Straggler: A slow process or GPU that causes others to wait.

Checkpoint: Saved training state used for recovery or resuming training.

Data parallelism: Replicate model, split data.

Model parallelism: Split model across GPUs.

Tensor parallelism: Split individual tensor operations across GPUs.

Pipeline parallelism: Split layers into stages and stream micro-batches.

Mixed precision: Use lower precision such as FP16/BF16 to reduce memory and improve speed.

docs.title: 39. Summary
docs.text:
GPU training starts with a simple idea: use GPUs for parallel tensor computation. Single-GPU training is straightforward, but scaling to multiple GPUs requires coordinating multiple devices.

The most common modern approach is PyTorch DistributedDataParallel, where each GPU is controlled by its own process, each process trains on different data, and gradients are synchronized every training step using NCCL collectives such as AllReduce.

Multi-GPU training can happen within one node or across many nodes. The mental model remains similar, but multi-node training introduces network communication and more failure modes. DDP is synchronous, so slow or failed workers affect the whole job. Practical robustness comes from good resource allocation, balanced data loading, monitoring, and checkpointing.

For very large models, data parallelism may not be enough. Techniques such as model parallelism, tensor parallelism, pipeline parallelism, mixed precision, and sharding are used to fit and train models at scale.

docs.title: 40. One-Line Summary
docs.text: GPU training accelerates deep learning by parallelizing tensor operations, and multi-GPU training scales this by distributing work across GPUs while synchronizing model updates through mechanisms such as PyTorch DDP and NCCL AllReduce.

docs.title: Autoregressive Language Modeling: Decoder-Only Transformer Architectures
1. Mathematical Framework
docs.text:
A Transformer is a neural network architecture for sequence modeling. It was originally introduced with an encoder-decoder structure, but many modern generative language models use a decoder-only version.

A decoder-only Transformer learns the following task:

Given previous tokens, predict the next token.

Mathematically:

P(next_token | previous_tokens)

This is called autoregressive modeling because the model generates one token at a time, feeding its own output back into the input context.

docs.title: 2. Transformer Family: Encoder, Decoder, Encoder-Decoder
docs.text:
At a high level:

  • Encoder-only models are good for understanding tasks.
  • Example use cases: classification, embeddings, retrieval, sentence similarity.
  • Encoder-decoder models are good for sequence-to-sequence tasks.
  • Example use cases: translation, summarization, structured generation.
  • Decoder-only models are good for open-ended generation.
  • Example use cases: chatbots, code generation, text completion.
flowchart LR
    A[Transformer Architectures] --> B[Encoder-only]
    A --> C[Encoder-Decoder]
    A --> D[Decoder-only]

    B --> B1[Understanding / Classification]
    C --> C1[Translation / Summarization]
    D --> D1[Autoregressive Generation]

This note focuses mainly on decoder-only Transformers.

docs.title: 3. Decoder-Only Transformer Pipeline
docs.text:
A decoder-only Transformer follows this conceptual flow:

flowchart LR
    A[Input Text] --> B[Tokenization]
    B --> C[Token IDs]
    C --> D[Token Embeddings]
    D --> E[Add Positional Information]
    E --> F[Transformer Decoder Blocks]
    F --> G[Output Logits]
    G --> H[Softmax]
    H --> I[Next Token Prediction]

In plain language:

Text → tokens → vectors → attention layers → probabilities → next token

docs.title: 4. Tokenization
docs.text:
Neural networks do not directly understand raw text. Text is first converted into tokens, and each token is mapped to an integer ID.

Example:

"I love machine learning"

might become:

["I", "love", "machine", "learning"]

and then:

[17, 482, 3912, 8401]

Modern language models often use subword tokenization, where uncommon words may be split into smaller pieces.

Example:

"unbelievable" → ["un", "believ", "able"]

This helps the model handle rare words, new words, and multiple languages without needing a vocabulary entry for every possible word.

docs.title: 5. Embedding Layer
docs.text:
Token IDs are just arbitrary integers. The model needs to convert them into meaningful numerical vectors.

The embedding layer maps each token ID to a dense vector:

token_id → embedding vector

Example:

17 → [0.12, -0.03, 0.88, ..., 0.41]

If the model hidden dimension is d_model = 4096, each token becomes a vector of length 4096.

So an input sequence of length N becomes a matrix:

N tokens × d_model dimensions

Example shape:

sequence_length = 128
hidden_dimension = 4096

Embedding output shape = 128 × 4096

docs.title: 6. Positional Information
docs.text:
A Transformer processes tokens in parallel, so it does not naturally know token order.

For example, these two sentences contain the same words but mean different things:

The dog chased the cat.
The cat chased the dog.

To encode order, positional information is added to token embeddings.

input_representation = token_embedding + positional_embedding

Conceptually:

flowchart LR
    A[Token ID] --> B[Token Embedding]
    C[Position Index] --> D[Position Embedding]
    B --> E[Add]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Input Vector to Transformer Block]

Modern models may use learned positional embeddings or variants such as rotary positional embeddings, but the high-level purpose is the same: give the model information about order and relative position.

docs.title: 7. Decoder Block Overview
docs.text:
A decoder-only Transformer is built by stacking many similar blocks.

Each block contains:

  1. Masked self-attention
  2. Feedforward neural network
  3. Residual connections
  4. Layer normalization

A simplified decoder block looks like this:

flowchart TD
    A[Input Hidden States] --> B[Masked Self-Attention]
    B --> C[Add + LayerNorm]
    C --> D[Feedforward Network]
    D --> E[Add + LayerNorm]
    E --> F[Output Hidden States]

The complete model repeats this block many times:

Embedding → Block 1 → Block 2 → Block 3 → ... → Block N → Output

docs.title: 8. Self-Attention: Core Intuition
docs.text:
Self-attention lets each token look at other tokens and decide which ones are important.

For example, in the sentence:

The animal did not cross the street because it was tired.

The word it needs to understand what it refers to. Attention helps the model connect it to relevant previous tokens such as animal.

The key idea:

Each token builds a new representation by mixing information from other tokens.

docs.title: 9. Query, Key, and Value
docs.text:
In self-attention, each token produces three vectors:

  • Query (Q): what this token is looking for
  • Key (K): what this token offers for matching
  • Value (V): the information this token contributes

A useful analogy:

Query = question
Key   = label/index
Value = content

The attention score between two tokens is computed using a dot product:

attention_score = Q · K

If the query of one token matches the key of another token strongly, the attention score is high.

docs.title: 10. Self-Attention Step by Step
docs.text:
For each token:

  1. Create Q, K, V vectors.
  2. Compare Q with all K vectors.
  3. Convert scores to probabilities using softmax.
  4. Use those probabilities to compute a weighted sum of V vectors.
flowchart TD
    A[Token Representations] --> B[Linear Projection to Q]
    A --> C[Linear Projection to K]
    A --> D[Linear Projection to V]
    B --> E[QKᵀ Attention Scores]
    C --> E
    E --> F[Scale + Mask]
    F --> G[Softmax Attention Weights]
    G --> H[Weighted Sum of V]
    D --> H
    H --> I[Contextualized Token Representations]

The common attention formula is:

Attention(Q, K, V) = softmax(QKᵀ / sqrt(d_k)) V

Where:

  • Q = queries
  • K = keys
  • V = values
  • d_k = key/query dimension
  • sqrt(d_k) scaling keeps dot products numerically stable

docs.title: 11. Causal Masking in Decoder-Only Models
docs.text:
Decoder-only models must not look into the future during training.

If the input is:

The cat sat on the

and the model is predicting the next token after cat, it should only see:

The cat

It should not see:

sat on the

This is enforced using a causal mask.

Token position i can only attend to positions ≤ i.

Visual example:

Allowed attention matrix

          attends to →
          T1  T2  T3  T4
Token T1   ✓   x   x   x
Token T2   ✓   ✓   x   x
Token T3   ✓   ✓   ✓   x
Token T4   ✓   ✓   ✓   ✓

This is why decoder attention is often called masked self-attention.

docs.title: 12. Multi-Head Attention
docs.text:
Instead of doing attention once, Transformers use multiple attention heads in parallel.

Each head can learn different types of relationships.

Examples:

  • one head may track subject-verb relationships
  • another may track nearby words
  • another may track long-range dependencies
  • another may focus on punctuation or structure
flowchart LR
    A[Input Hidden States] --> H1[Attention Head 1]
    A --> H2[Attention Head 2]
    A --> H3[Attention Head 3]
    A --> H4[Attention Head 4]
    H1 --> C[Concatenate]
    H2 --> C
    H3 --> C
    H4 --> C
    C --> O[Output Projection]

The outputs of all heads are concatenated and projected back to the model hidden dimension.

High-level intuition:

Single-head attention = one way of looking at context
Multi-head attention = many different ways of looking at context

docs.title: 13. Attention Complexity
docs.text:
Self-attention is powerful but expensive.

If a sequence has length N, each token compares itself to every other token.

That means the attention score matrix has size:

N × N

So the complexity is approximately:

O(N²)

Example:

N = 1,000 tokens  → 1,000,000 attention interactions
N = 10,000 tokens → 100,000,000 attention interactions

This quadratic growth affects both compute and memory.

flowchart LR
    A[Sequence Length N] --> B[Attention Matrix N × N]
    B --> C[Compute Cost O(N²)]
    B --> D[Memory Cost O(N²)]

This is one reason long-context models are expensive and why efficient attention methods are an active area of research.

docs.title: 14. Feedforward Network
docs.text:
After attention, each token goes through a feedforward neural network.

A typical FFN looks like:

Linear → Activation → Linear

Often the hidden dimension expands inside the FFN.

Example:

d_model = 4096
ffn_hidden = 16384

So the FFN may expand the representation and then project it back down.

Important distinction:

Attention mixes information across tokens.
FFN processes each token independently.

flowchart TD
    A[Token 1] --> F1[Same FFN]
    B[Token 2] --> F2[Same FFN]
    C[Token 3] --> F3[Same FFN]
    F1 --> O1[Updated Token 1]
    F2 --> O2[Updated Token 2]
    F3 --> O3[Updated Token 3]

The FFN is shared across positions, but applied separately to each token.

docs.title: 15. Residual Connections and Layer Normalization
docs.text:
Deep Transformers are difficult to train without stabilizing components.

Two important tools are:

  • Residual connections
  • Layer normalization

A residual connection adds the input back to the output of a sublayer:

x_new = x + sublayer(x)

Layer normalization stabilizes the scale of activations.

Conceptually:

x → Attention → Add original x → Normalize
x → FFN       → Add original x → Normalize

These components help gradients flow through very deep networks.

docs.title: 16. Output Layer and Next Token Prediction
docs.text:
After the final Transformer block, each token has a final hidden representation.

The model projects the final hidden state into vocabulary space:

hidden_state → logits over vocabulary

If vocabulary size is 50,000, the model outputs 50,000 scores for the next token.

Then softmax converts scores into probabilities:

logits → probabilities

Example:

P("cat")  = 0.30
P("dog")  = 0.22
P("car")  = 0.04
P("tree") = 0.01
...

The next token is selected from this distribution.

docs.title: 17. Training Objective
docs.text:
Decoder-only models are trained with next-token prediction.

Example training sequence:

Input:  The cat sat on the
Target: cat sat on the mat

At every position, the model predicts the next token.

The       → cat
The cat   → sat
The cat sat → on
The cat sat on → the
The cat sat on the → mat

The loss is usually cross-entropy loss over the vocabulary.

docs.title: 18. Autoregressive Generation
docs.text:
During inference, the model generates one token at a time.

flowchart TD
    A[Prompt] --> B[Predict Next Token]
    B --> C[Append Token to Prompt]
    C --> D[Run Model Again]
    D --> B

Example:

Step 1: "The"
Step 2: "The cat"
Step 3: "The cat sat"
Step 4: "The cat sat on"
Step 5: "The cat sat on the"

This process continues until:

  • an end token is generated
  • a maximum length is reached
  • a stopping rule is triggered

docs.title: 19. Why Decoder-Only Models Are Popular for Generation
docs.text:
Decoder-only models are especially natural for generation because their training objective matches their inference behavior.

Training:

predict next token from previous tokens

Inference:

generate next token from previous tokens

This alignment makes decoder-only models simple, scalable, and effective for open-ended language generation.

docs.title: 20. Mixture of Experts
docs.text:
A standard dense Transformer uses all model parameters for every token.

This can become very expensive as models grow.

Mixture of Experts (MoE) changes this by replacing some dense feedforward layers with multiple expert networks.

A router decides which experts should process each token.

flowchart TD
    A[Token Representation] --> R[Router]
    R --> E1[Expert 1]
    R --> E2[Expert 2]
    R -. not selected .-> E3[Expert 3]
    R -. not selected .-> E4[Expert 4]
    E1 --> C[Combine Outputs]
    E2 --> C
    C --> O[Updated Token Representation]

Example:

64 total experts
only 2 experts used per token

This means the model can have many parameters, but only a subset is active for each token.

Benefits:

  • larger model capacity
  • lower compute per token than a fully dense model of similar total size
  • expert specialization

Tradeoffs:

  • routing complexity
  • load balancing issues
  • harder distributed training
  • communication overhead

Simple intuition:

Dense model: every token consults the whole committee.
MoE model: every token consults a few specialists.

docs.title: 21. Training vs Inference: Important Difference
docs.text:
Training processes many tokens in parallel and computes gradients.

Inference generates tokens sequentially.

Training:

many sequences → forward → loss → backward → update

Inference:

prompt → predict one token → append → repeat

During inference, models often use a KV cache to avoid recomputing keys and values for previous tokens at every generation step.

Without KV cache:

Recompute attention history every step

With KV cache:

Reuse previous K/V tensors and compute only for new token

This makes autoregressive generation much faster.

docs.title: 22. Why Transformers Need GPUs
docs.text:
Transformers are dominated by large tensor operations:

  • matrix multiplications in attention
  • matrix multiplications in FFN layers
  • large embedding and output projection operations

GPUs are well-suited because they can execute many operations in parallel.

However, Transformers can still be limited by:

  • GPU memory capacity
  • memory bandwidth
  • attention memory cost
  • communication overhead during multi-GPU training

This is why large Transformer training often uses:

  • data parallelism
  • tensor/model parallelism
  • pipeline parallelism
  • mixed precision
  • checkpointing
  • distributed training systems

docs.title: 23. Simple Mental Model
docs.text:
A decoder-only Transformer can be summarized as:

1. Convert text into tokens.
2. Convert tokens into vectors.
3. Add position information.
4. Let tokens attend to previous tokens.
5. Process each token with an FFN.
6. Repeat many times.
7. Predict the next token.
8. Append the token and repeat.

Even shorter:

Tokens become vectors, vectors exchange context through attention, and the model predicts the next token.

docs.title: 24. Key Terms Glossary
docs.text:
Token: A unit of text, often a word or subword.

Embedding: A dense vector representation of a token.

Position embedding: Information added so the model knows token order.

Self-attention: Mechanism where tokens attend to other tokens in the same sequence.

Causal mask: Prevents a token from seeing future tokens.

Attention head: One attention mechanism inside multi-head attention.

Multi-head attention: Multiple attention heads operating in parallel.

FFN: Feedforward network applied independently to each token.

Residual connection: Adds input back to output to stabilize deep networks.

LayerNorm: Normalizes activations to improve training stability.

Logits: Raw output scores before softmax.

Softmax: Converts logits into probabilities.

Autoregressive generation: Generating one token at a time using previous tokens.

MoE: Mixture of Experts; activates only selected expert networks per token.

KV cache: Cached keys and values used to speed up inference.

docs.title: 25. One-Line Summary
docs.text: A decoder-only Transformer generates text by converting tokens into vectors, repeatedly using masked self-attention to build context-aware representations, and predicting the next token autoregressively.

docs.title: Geospatial & Remote Sensing Notes
docs.text: These indices and articles compile core reference logs on coordinate reference systems, raster IO structures, state estimation algorithms (Kalman Filters) used in localization, and foundational hyperspectral physics.

docs.title: 📖 Key Reference Journals & Guides
docs.text:
• Bidhya's Geospatial Python Stack: Standard import guide and optimized reference codes for GeoPandas, Rasterio, Rioxarray, and Xarray.
  • Kalman Filter & LiDAR Fusion: State estimation mathematics, predict/update loop configurations, and vehicle localization sensor alignments.
  • Hyperspectral Remote Sensing Theory: Differences between continuous narrow-band hyperspectral arrays (e.g., AVIRIS, PACE) vs discrete broad-band multispectral sensors.

docs.title: Methodological Priorities
docs.text: In geospatial workflows, my emphasis is on physical accuracy mixed with numerical efficiency: * Grid Consistency: Handling mismatched spatial resolutions and projections using highly-parallelised rioxarray or gdal abstractions. * Dynamic Range Correctness: Empirical satellite bias corrections using satellite altimetry footprints (e.g., ICESat-2) to validate Digital Elevation Models.

docs.title: Geospatial Analysis and Multi-Dimensional Data Pipelines: Vector and Raster Reference Guide
docs.text: This reference outlines production conventions and optimized design patterns for handling high-resolution satellite imagery, spatiotemporal array telemetry, and vector layers in Python.

docs.title: Core Architectural Library Stack
docs.text:
# ============================================================
# CORE IMPORTS - STANDARD PRODUCTION STACK
# ============================================================

# VECTOR OPERATIONS
import geopandas as gpd
from shapely.geometry import Point, Polygon, box

# RASTER OPERATIONS  
import rioxarray                 # Optimized index and coordinate-aware raster I/O

# GRIDDED / SPATIOTEMPORAL DATA (NetCDF, GRIB, Zarr)
import xarray as xr

# MATRICES & COMPUTATIONAL UTILITIES
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

# FILE & SYSTEM OPERATIONS
import requests
import os                        # Standard OS and file-system path operations

# ============================================================
# SUBSYSTEM BACKUPS (Reserved for legacy / low-level integrations)
# ============================================================
# import rasterio                # Use only if rioxarray won't work
# import gdal                    # Last resort only

docs.title: Check Existence
docs.text:
import os

# Check if file exists
if os.path.exists('data.tif'):
    print("File found!")

# Check if directory
if os.path.isdir('output'):
    print("Directory exists")

docs.title: Create Directories
docs.text:
# Create single directory
if not os.path.exists('output'):
    os.mkdir('output')

# Create nested directories
os.makedirs('output/results/maps', exist_ok=True)

docs.title: Path Operations
docs.text:
# Join paths (cross-platform)
file_path = os.path.join('data', 'rasters', 'dem.tif')

# Get absolute path
abs_path = os.path.abspath('data.tif')

# Split path
directory, filename = os.path.split('/path/to/file.tif')
name, ext = os.path.splitext('file.tif')  # ('file', '.tif')

# Get components
dirname = os.path.dirname('/path/to/file.tif')   # '/path/to'
basename = os.path.basename('/path/to/file.tif') # 'file.tif'

# File size
size = os.path.getsize('data.tif')  # Bytes

# List directory
files = os.listdir('data')

docs.title: Read Raster
docs.text:
import rioxarray

# Read raster
da = rioxarray.open_rasterio('dem.tif')

# Inspect metadata
print(f"CRS: {da.rio.crs}")
print(f"Bounds: {da.rio.bounds()}")
print(f"Resolution: {da.rio.resolution()}")
print(f"Nodata: {da.rio.nodata}")
print(f"Shape: {da.shape}")

# Access as NumPy array
data = da.values

# Lazy loading for big data
da_lazy = rioxarray.open_rasterio('big.tif', chunks={'band': 1, 'y': 1024, 'x': 1024})

docs.title: Write/Save Raster
docs.text:
# Save raster
da.rio.to_raster('output.tif')

# With compression
da.rio.to_raster('output.tif', compress='lzw')

docs.title: Reproject Raster
docs.text:
# Reproject to different CRS (ONE LINE!)
da_reprojected = da.rio.reproject('EPSG:4326', resampling=5)  # 5=bilinear

# Reproject to match another raster (CRS + extent + resolution)
da_matched = da.rio.reproject_match(other_da)

# Resampling methods:
# 0 = nearest (for categorical: land cover, soil type)
# 5 = bilinear (for continuous: elevation, temperature)
# 3 = cubic (smoother, slower)

docs.title: Clip/Mask Raster
docs.text:
# Clip to polygon extent
da_clipped = da.rio.clip(
    polygon_gdf.geometry,
    polygon_gdf.crs,
    drop=True,             # Crop to polygon extent
    all_touched=False      # Only pixels with centers inside
)

docs.title: Handle Nodata
docs.text:
# Mask nodata values
da_valid = da.where(da != da.rio.nodata)

# Replace nodata with NaN

docs.title: Zonal Statistics
docs.text:
import rioxarray

da = rioxarray.open_rasterio('raster.tif')
polygons_gdf = gpd.read_file('polygons.shp')

# Ensure CRS match
if polygons_gdf.crs != da.rio.crs:
    polygons_gdf = polygons_gdf.to_crs(da.rio.crs)

# Calculate stats for each polygon
for idx, polygon in polygons_gdf.iterrows():
    # Clip raster to polygon
    clipped = da.rio.clip([polygon.geometry], polygons_gdf.crs, drop=True)

    # Handle nodata
    if da.rio.nodata is not None:
        clipped_valid = clipped.where(clipped != da.rio.nodata)
    else:
        clipped_valid = clipped

    # Calculate statistics
    polygons_gdf.loc[idx, 'mean'] = float(clipped_valid.mean().values)
    polygons_gdf.loc[idx, 'min'] = float(clipped_valid.min().values)
    polygons_gdf.loc[idx, 'max'] = float(clipped_valid.max().values)
    polygons_gdf.loc[idx, 'std'] = float(clipped_valid.std().values)
    polygons_gdf.loc[idx, 'count'] = int(clipped_valid.count().values)

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Theory notes for hyperspectral algorithms and concepts. Reference material for Matter Intelligence engineering reference.

docs.title: Multispectral (Your Current Expertise)
docs.text:
• Bands: 3-20 discrete spectral bands
  • Bandwidth: 50-200 nm (broad bands)
  • Examples: Sentinel-2 (13 bands), Landsat 8 (11 bands), MODIS (36 bands)
  • Applications: Land cover classification, vegetation monitoring, water quality
  • Your Experience: ✅ Extensive (6+ years)

docs.title: Hyperspectral (Learning Now)
docs.text:
• Bands: 100-300+ continuous spectral bands
  • Bandwidth: 5-10 nm (narrow bands)
  • Examples: AVIRIS (224 bands), Hyperion (220 bands), Matter's ultraspectral sensor
  • Applications: Mineral identification, precision agriculture, chemical detection, material ID
  • Your Experience: ⚠️ Limited hands-on, strong conceptual foundation

docs.title: Key Differences
docs.text: Aspect Multispectral Hyperspectral Spectral sampling Discrete Continuous Band width Broad (50-200nm) Narrow (5-10nm) Number of bands 3-50 100-300+ Primary use Land cover, indices Material identification Data volume Moderate Large Processing complexity Lower Higher

docs.title: What Transfers Directly
docs.text: ✅ Spectral analysis principles (band math, radiometry) ✅ Atmospheric correction concepts ✅ Classification workflows (supervised/unsupervised) ✅ Validation methods (ground truth, accuracy assessment) ✅ Data structures (3D arrays, xarray operations) ✅ Production pipelines (AWS, parallelization, optimization)

docs.title: Concept
docs.text: Measures spectral similarity as the angle between two vectors in n-dimensional spectral space.

docs.title: Mathematical Formula
docs.text:
SAM(pixel, reference) = arccos( (pixel · reference) / (||pixel|| × ||reference||) )

Where: - · = dot product - ||vector|| = Euclidean norm (magnitude) - Result in radians (0 to π/2)

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text:
• Geometrically: Angle between two vectors in n-dimensional space (n = number of bands)
  • Smaller angle = more similar spectra
  • Threshold: Classify if angle < threshold (e.g., 0.1 radians)

docs.title: Advantages
docs.text:
1. Illumination invariant: Normalization removes brightness effects
  2. Simple to implement: Just dot product and norms
  3. Fast computation: Vectorizes well for large images
  4. Interpretable: Angle has physical meaning

docs.title: Disadvantages
docs.text:
1. Ignores magnitude: Bright and dark versions of same material look identical
  2. No statistical basis: Unlike Mahalanobis distance or SVM
  3. Fixed threshold: Requires manual tuning

docs.title: Python Implementation (Conceptual)
docs.text:
import numpy as np

def spectral_angle_mapper(pixel_spectrum, reference_spectrum):
    """
    Calculate SAM angle between pixel and reference spectrum.

    Args:
        pixel_spectrum: 1D array of reflectance values (n_bands,)
        reference_spectrum: 1D array of reference reflectance (n_bands,)

    Returns:
        angle: SAM angle in radians
    """
    # Dot product
    dot_product = np.dot(pixel_spectrum, reference_spectrum)

    # Norms (magnitudes)
    norm_pixel = np.linalg.norm(pixel_spectrum)
    norm_ref = np.linalg.norm(reference_spectrum)

    # Cosine of angle
    cos_angle = dot_product / (norm_pixel * norm_ref)

    # Clip to valid range [-1, 1] to avoid numerical errors
    cos_angle = np.clip(cos_angle, -1, 1)

    # Angle in radians
    angle = np.arccos(cos_angle)

    return angle

# For full image classification:
# sam_map = np.array([[spectral_angle_mapper(cube[i,j,:], ref) 
#                      for j in range(cols)] for i in range(rows)])

docs.title: Interview Talking Point
docs.text: "SAM measures spectral similarity as the angle between vectors in n-dimensional space—it's essentially cosine similarity, which I've used in ML feature matching. The normalization makes it insensitive to illumination variations, which is valuable for remote sensing where brightness can vary due to topography or atmospheric conditions."

docs.title: Concept
docs.text: Each pixel's reflectance is a mixture of multiple materials (endmembers). Unmixing decomposes the pixel into fractional abundances of each endmember.

docs.title: Linear Mixing Model
docs.text:
R_pixel(λ) = Σ [f_i × R_i(λ)] + ε(λ)

Where:
- R_pixel(λ) = observed pixel reflectance at wavelength λ
- R_i(λ) = endmember i reflectance at wavelength λ
- f_i = fractional abundance of endmember i (0 ≤ f_i ≤ 1)
- ε(λ) = error/noise

docs.title: Constraints
docs.text:
1. Sum-to-one: Σ f_i = 1 (abundances sum to 100%)
  2. Non-negativity: f_i ≥ 0 (can't have negative fractions)

docs.title: Linear Spectral Unmixing
docs.text:
• Assumption: Photons interact with only one material before reaching sensor (surface scattering)
  • Method: Solve constrained least squares problem
  • When valid: Most remote sensing scenarios

docs.title: Nonlinear Unmixing
docs.text:
• Assumption: Photons interact with multiple materials (multiple scattering)
  • Method: More complex optimization (neural networks, kernel methods)
  • When needed: Dense vegetation canopies, complex scenes

docs.title: 1. Endmember Extraction
docs.text:
Goal: Identify pure material spectra (endmembers)

Methods: - PPI (Pixel Purity Index): Find extreme pixels in convex hull - N-FINDR: Maximize volume of simplex in spectral space - VCA (Vertex Component Analysis): Geometric approach - Manual selection: Use known pure pixels or spectral library

docs.title: 2. Abundance Estimation
docs.text:
Goal: Solve for fractional abundances given endmembers

Methods: - Unconstrained Least Squares (LSU): Fast but can violate constraints - Non-Negative Least Squares (NNLS): Enforces f_i ≥ 0 - Fully Constrained Least Squares (FCLS): Enforces sum-to-one + non-negativity

docs.title: 3. Validation
docs.text:
• Check residual error (how well model fits observed spectrum)
  • Verify abundances make physical sense
  • Compare to ground truth if available

docs.title: Python Implementation (Conceptual)
docs.text:
from scipy.optimize import nnls
import numpy as np

def linear_unmixing_nnls(pixel_spectrum, endmembers):
    """
    Unmix pixel using Non-Negative Least Squares.

    Args:
        pixel_spectrum: 1D array (n_bands,)
        endmembers: 2D array (n_endmembers, n_bands)

    Returns:
        abundances: 1D array (n_endmembers,) - fractional abundances
    """
    # Solve: minimize ||pixel - Σ(abundance_i × endmember_i)||^2
    # subject to: abundance_i ≥ 0
    abundances, residual = nnls(endmembers.T, pixel_spectrum)

    # Optional: normalize to sum-to-one
    abundances_normalized = abundances / abundances.sum()

    return abundances_normalized

# For full image:
# abundance_maps = np.zeros((rows, cols, n_endmembers))
# for i in range(rows):
#     for j in range(cols):
#         abundance_maps[i, j, :] = linear_unmixing_nnls(cube[i,j,:], endmembers)

docs.title: Interview Talking Point
docs.text: "Spectral unmixing is essentially a constrained linear regression problem—you're fitting observed spectra as weighted sums of reference endmember spectra. I've solved similar optimization problems in ML. The constraints (non-negativity, sum-to-one) make physical sense: you can't have negative fractions, and the pixel must be 100% something. I'd use scipy's NNLS or cvxpy for convex optimization."

docs.title: Why Reduce Dimensionality?
docs.text:
1. Noise: Not all bands contain useful signal
  2. Redundancy: Adjacent hyperspectral bands highly correlated
  3. Computational cost: 200+ bands = expensive processing
  4. Curse of dimensionality: ML classifiers struggle in high dimensions

docs.title: Concept
docs.text: Transform correlated bands into uncorrelated principal components ordered by variance explained.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Standardize data (mean=0, variance=1)
  2. Compute covariance matrix
  3. Compute eigenvectors and eigenvalues
  4. Sort by eigenvalue (variance explained)
  5. Project data onto top k eigenvectors

docs.title: Output
docs.text:
• PC1: Direction of maximum variance (often brightness)
  • PC2: Second-most variance (often greenness)
  • PC3+: Progressively less variance (often noise)

docs.title: Typical Result
docs.text:
• First 5-10 PCs capture 95%+ of variance
  • Remaining 200+ PCs mostly noise

docs.title: Python (Scikit-learn)
docs.text:
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA

# Reshape cube to 2D: (n_pixels, n_bands)
pixels = hypercube.reshape(-1, n_bands)

# Apply PCA
pca = PCA(n_components=10)  # Keep 10 components
pcs = pca.fit_transform(pixels)

# Reshape back to image
pc_cube = pcs.reshape(n_rows, n_cols, 10)

print(f"Variance explained: {pca.explained_variance_ratio_}")

docs.title: Concept
docs.text: PCA variant that separates signal from noise by maximizing SNR (Signal-to-Noise Ratio).

docs.title: Difference from PCA
docs.text:
• PCA: Maximizes total variance (signal + noise)
  • MNF: Maximizes signal variance relative to noise variance

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Estimate noise covariance matrix (from dark pixels or repeat observations)
  2. Apply noise whitening transformation
  3. Apply PCA to whitened data
  4. Invert transformation

docs.title: Output
docs.text:
• Early MNF components: High SNR (signal-dominated)
  • Late MNF components: Low SNR (noise-dominated)

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
• MNF better than PCA when noise levels vary by band (common in hyperspectral)
  • PCA sufficient for many applications

docs.title: Interview Talking Point
docs.text: "Dimensionality reduction is standard ML practice—PCA for hyperspectral is the same principle I apply to feature engineering. Going from 224 bands to 10 principal components reduces computational cost while retaining 95%+ of the signal. MNF is a refinement that accounts for band-specific noise, which is smart for hyperspectral sensors with variable SNR across the spectrum."

docs.title: Problem
docs.text:
At-sensor radiance ≠ Surface reflectance

Atmosphere affects measurements through: 1. Scattering: Rayleigh (molecules), Mie (aerosols), non-selective (large particles) 2. Absorption: Water vapor, O₂, O₃, CO₂

docs.title: Goal
docs.text: Remove atmospheric effects to recover surface reflectance (what we actually care about).

docs.title: MODTRAN (MODerate resolution atmospheric TRANsmission)
docs.text:
• Type: Physics-based radiative transfer model
  • Status: Gold standard, very complex
  • Inputs: Atmospheric profile (pressure, temperature, water vapor, aerosols), viewing geometry, solar angles
  • Use: Research, high-accuracy applications
  • Your Positioning: "Know it's the gold standard; would use existing implementations or libraries"

docs.title: 6S (Second Simulation of Satellite Signal in the Solar Spectrum)
docs.text:
• Type: Radiative transfer model (simpler than MODTRAN)
  • Status: Open-source, widely used in research
  • Inputs: Similar to MODTRAN but fewer parameters
  • Use: Academic research, open-source projects
  • Python: Py6S package available

docs.title: FLAASH (Fast Line-of-sight Atmospheric Analysis of Spectral Hypercubes)
docs.text:
• Type: Commercial implementation (ENVI software)
  • Status: Industry standard for hyperspectral correction
  • Use: Operational processing, commercial applications

docs.title: Empirical Line Calibration (ELC)
docs.text:
• Type: Simple empirical method
  • Approach: Use ground targets with known reflectance to calibrate
  • Requirements: At least 2 targets (bright and dark) with known spectra
  • Advantage: No atmospheric profile needed
  • Disadvantage: Less accurate, spatially variable if atmospheric conditions change

docs.title: Simplified Equation (Conceptual)
docs.text:
L_sensor = (L_surface × τ) + L_path

Where:
- L_sensor = at-sensor radiance (what satellite measures)
- L_surface = surface radiance (what we want)
- τ = atmospheric transmittance (0-1)
- L_path = path radiance (atmospheric scattering adding signal)

docs.title: Interview Talking Points
docs.text:
Honest Positioning:

"I've worked extensively with atmospherically corrected products—MODIS MOD09, Sentinel-2 Level-2A surface reflectance. I understand the physics: Rayleigh scattering (blue sky), water vapor absorption bands, aerosol effects.

For hyperspectral-specific correction, I know MODTRAN is the gold standard and 6S is commonly used in research (Py6S exists). I haven't run these models from scratch, but I can use existing implementations or libraries. For rapid prototyping, empirical line calibration is straightforward if ground targets are available.

In production, I'd leverage existing correction pipelines or collaborate with atmospheric scientists rather than reinventing radiative transfer models—focusing my effort on the algorithm development and validation where I add more value."

Technical Depth (If Pushed): - "Atmospheric correction workflow: (1) radiometric calibration to at-sensor radiance, (2) atmospheric modeling or empirical calibration, (3) conversion to surface reflectance" - "Key atmospheric effects: Rayleigh scattering (wavelength^-4), water vapor absorption (~1400nm, 1900nm), ozone absorption (~600nm)" - "Validation: Compare corrected reflectance to field spectrometer measurements"

  • Bands: Laboratory measurements, 0.4-2.5 μm (hyperspectral resolution)
  • Access: Free, public (https://www.usgs.gov/labs/spec-lab)

docs.text:

  • Bands: 0.4-15 μm (includes thermal)

  • Native Resolution: Matches AVIRIS sensor

docs.title: Field Spectrometry

  • Use: Collect in-situ measurements for validation
  • Workflow: Measure reference targets during satellite overpass

docs.title: Interview Talking Point

For Matter's applications, I'd work with field spectroscopy teams to collect in-situ measurements during sensor acquisitions to validate products and build material-specific libraries."

docs.title: 1. Field Spectrometry
docs.text:
• Method: Handheld spectrometer measurements during overpass
  • Advantage: Direct spectral validation
  • Challenge: Spatial mismatch (point vs. pixel), timing synchronization

docs.title: 2. Reference Datasets
docs.text:
• Higher Resolution Imagery: Compare to commercial 1-2m imagery
  • Existing Products: Compare to validated products (e.g., USGS mineral maps)
  • In-Situ Sampling: Lab analysis of collected samples

docs.title: 3. Physics Checks
docs.text:
• Reflectance Range: Should be 0-1 (or 0-100%)
  • Spectral Shape: Does it match expected material signature?
  • Endmember Abundances: Do they sum to ~1? Are they non-negative?

docs.title: Classification Accuracy
docs.text:
• Confusion Matrix: Class-by-class performance
  • Overall Accuracy: (Correct predictions) / (Total predictions)
  • Producer's Accuracy: How often ground truth class correctly predicted
  • User's Accuracy: How often prediction is correct
  • Kappa Coefficient: Agreement beyond chance

docs.title: Regression (Continuous Values)
docs.text:
• RMSE: Root Mean Square Error
  • Bias: Systematic over/under-estimation
  • R²: Coefficient of determination (variance explained)

docs.title: Cross-Validation
docs.text:
• Spatial CV: Train/test split by geographic location (avoid spatial autocorrelation)
  • Temporal CV: Train on one acquisition, test on another
  • K-fold CV: Standard ML practice

docs.title: Interview Talking Point
docs.text: "Rigorous validation is my standard practice—I validate every data product against ground truth. For hyperspectral, that means field spectrometry, reference spectral libraries, and physics consistency checks. I use statistical metrics (RMSE, confusion matrices, R²) and spatial cross-validation to ensure results are robust. Published peer-reviewed accuracy assessments demonstrate I take validation seriously."

docs.title: End-to-End Pipeline
docs.text:
1. Data Acquisition
   ├─ Hyperspectral sensor (airborne/spaceborne)
   └─ Ground truth (field spectrometry, sampling)

2. Preprocessing
   ├─ Radiometric calibration (DN → radiance)
   ├─ Atmospheric correction (radiance → surface reflectance)
   ├─ Geometric correction (orthorectification)
   └─ Noise reduction (optional: smoothing, destriping)

3. Dimensionality Reduction (optional)
   ├─ PCA or MNF to reduce bands
   └─ Improves computational efficiency

4. Analysis
   ├─ Spectral indices (vegetation, mineral, water)
   ├─ Classification (SAM, SVM, Random Forest)
   ├─ Unmixing (endmember extraction → abundance mapping)
   └─ Change detection (temporal analysis)

5. Validation
   ├─ Compare to ground truth
   ├─ Statistical metrics (accuracy, RMSE)
   └─ Physics checks (sensible values?)

6. Delivery
   ├─ Classified maps, abundance maps, data products
   ├─ Uncertainty/confidence layers
   └─ Documentation and metadata

docs.title: Implementing Kalman Filters: A Geosensing Guide to State Estimation and LiDAR Fusion
docs.text: An engineering reference guide on the mathematical framework, covariance analysis, and multi-sensor fusion applications of Kalman filters in dynamic systems.

docs.title: 1. What Is a Kalman Filter?
docs.text: A mathematical algorithm that estimates the state of a dynamic system from noisy measurements over time.

docs.title: Core cycle (repeats continuously)
docs.text:
Step What happens Predict Estimate current state from previous state + motion model Update Correct prediction using new sensor measurement

The filter optimally weights predictions vs. measurements based on their respective uncertainties.

docs.title: 2a. Vehicle Localization
docs.text:
State vector: [x, y, z, velocity_x, velocity_y, heading, angular_velocity]

Prediction  → motion model (constant velocity / acceleration)
Update      → fuse LiDAR point cloud matching with GPS + IMU + wheel encoders

Result      → smooth, accurate position even with noisy/missing GPS

docs.title: 2b. Object Tracking
docs.text:
State vector: [object_x, object_y, velocity_x, velocity_y, acceleration]

Prediction  → extrapolate object motion between LiDAR scans
Update      → match LiDAR detections to predicted positions

Result      → consistent object tracks despite detection gaps or noise

docs.title: 2c. Multi-Sensor Fusion Pipeline
docs.text:
Sensor Strength Weakness LiDAR Accurate 3D positions Low frequency (10–20 Hz) IMU High-frequency motion (100+ Hz) Drifts over time GPS Absolute position Noisy; can drop out

Kalman filter strategy: - Use IMU for high-rate predictions - Correct with LiDAR / GPS measurements - Result: smooth, high-frequency, accurate state estimates

docs.title: 3. The Covariance Matrix — In Plain English
What it is
docs.text:
The covariance matrix is the filter's "confidence report card" — it tracks how certain the system is about each estimate and how uncertainties relate to each other.

Value Meaning Small number High confidence in this estimate Large number Low confidence — take with a grain of salt

It also captures relationships between variables: e.g. uncertainty in X position often correlates with uncertainty in X velocity.

docs.title: Real-world analogy — finding a friend in a mall
docs.text:
• GPS: "You're somewhere in this 10-metre circle" → high uncertainty
  • Landmark spotted: "Definitely near the food court" → low uncertainty
  • Text directions: "Walk 50 steps north" → medium uncertainty

The covariance matrix is your brain tracking how much to trust each source.

docs.title: How the filter uses it
docs.text: Phase What happens Prediction Confidence decreases: "I was sure 1s ago, but I've been moving" Update Filter weights sensors by reliability: LiDAR (precise) > GPS (noisy)

docs.title: Key takeaway
docs.text: The covariance matrix lets the filter automatically ask: "Here's my best guess — and here's exactly how sure I am about it."

docs.title: 4. Key Advantage for Autonomous Vehicles
docs.text: Kalman filters handle uncertainty quantification via covariance matrices — knowing when to trust predictions vs. measurements. This is essential in noisy sensor environments like AV stacks combining LiDAR, IMU, and GPS.

docs.title: 5. Production Considerations and Implementation Trade-offs
docs.text:
When deploying state estimation algorithms in real-time robotics, autonomous vehicle, or geospatial tracking pipelines, developers must address several engineering challenges:

  • Non-Linear State Transitions: Standard Kalman Filters (KF) assume linear relationships. Real-world systems require Extended Kalman Filters (EKF), which linearize non-linear state transitions using Jacobian matrices, or Unscented Kalman Filters (UKF), which use deterministic sigma points to capture the probability distribution. Selecting between EKF and UKF involves an engineering trade-off between computational cost and estimation accuracy.
  • Sensor Latency and Out-of-Sequence Measurements (OOSM): Sensors report data at vastly different frequencies and with varying propagation delays. Real-time fusion engines must maintain a chronological buffer of states and covariances to rewind, insert a delayed measurement, and re-propagate predictions.
  • Covariance Optimization (Tuning): Process noise \(Q\) and measurement noise \(R\) represent the mathematical representations of state physics uncertainty and sensor fidelity. Overestimating sensor reliability causes the filter to track noise, while underestimating it leads to lag. Tuning is usually achieved using system identification, statistical test runs, or optimization algorithms from flight/drive logs.

docs.title: Code Tutorials & Jupyter Notebooks
docs.text:
This folder holds fully-documented, interactive Jupyter Notebooks detailing end-to-end processing of satellite imagery and hyperspectral data arrays.

These notebooks are converted dynamically to webpage documents with all code output, figures, and maps rendered inline.

docs.text:

  • Sentinel-2 Change Detection: Preprocessing, spectral indexes calculations, classification pipelines, and change-detection maps using free Sentinel-2 imagery.

docs.title: Natural Language Processing (NLP) & GenAI
docs.text: In addition to physical modeling and computer vision, I apply deep learning to NLP problems to enable project discovery, metadata tagging, and literature classification: * Named Entity Recognition (NER) Foundations: Processing text with modern Transformer pipelines to extract domain entities, organizations, and spatial markers. * Topic Modeling & Semantic Structuring: Organizing unstructured scholarly logs, abstracts, or communications into coherent latent thematic models. * Sentiment Analysis & Zero-Shot Classification: Fusing pre-trained language models to tag and classify documents or query inputs on arbitrary criteria without prompt training.

docs.title: Running Notebooks Locally
docs.text:
All tutorials are run in isolated Python kernels handled through the pixi environment manager. To clone and host these notebooks on your machine:

  1. Clone this repository: 
    git clone https://github.com/bidhya/githubio.git
cd githubio
  2. Install dependencies and switch on the environment using pixi: 
    pixi run jupyter notebook

def aligned(a, b, tol=1.0):
    return (a.sizes == b.sizes and
            a.rio.crs == b.rio.crs and
            np.allclose(a.x.values, b.x.values, atol=tol) and
            np.allclose(a.y.values, b.y.values, atol=tol))

def change_map(before, after, name):
    """Encode 4-class change: before×2 + after (0–3)."""
    cm = (before * 2 + after).rename(name)
    return cm.compute()

change_ndwi = change_map(water_b_ndwi, water_a_ndwi_al, "change_ndwi")
change_sam  = change_map(water_b_sam,  water_a_sam_al,  "change_sam")
print("Change maps computed.")

def class_stats(change_da, pixel_km2=PIXEL_KM2):
    valid = ~np.isnan(change_da.values)
    n = valid.sum()
    rows = []
    for cls_id, cls_name in CHANGE_CLASSES.items():
        count = int((change_da.values[valid] == cls_id).sum())
        rows.append({
            "Class": cls_name,
            "Pixels": count,
            "Area km²": round(count * pixel_km2, 3),
            "%": round(100.0*count/n, 3) if n else 0.0,
        })
    return pd.DataFrame(rows)

print("\n── NDWI Change (full scene) ──────────────────────────────────────────")
df_ndwi = class_stats(change_ndwi)
print(df_ndwi.to_string(index=False))

def _safe(v):
    return None if (v is None or (isinstance(v, float) and (math.isnan(v) or math.isinf(v)))) else round(v, 4)

def summary(df, label):
    s = df.set_index("Class")["Area km²"]
    wg, wl, sw = s["Water Gain"], s["Water Loss"], s["Stable Water"]
    total_b = wg + sw
    net     = wg - wl
    pct     = 100.0 * net / total_b if total_b > 0 else float("nan")
    print(f"\n{label}")
    print(f"  Water Gain : {wg:.3f} km²   Water Loss: {wl:.3f} km²   Stable Water: {sw:.3f} km²")
    print(f"  Net change : {net:+.3f} km²  ({pct:+.1f}% relative to before)")
    return {"water_gain_km2": _safe(wg), "water_loss_km2": _safe(wl),
            "stable_water_km2": _safe(sw), "net_change_km2": _safe(net), "net_pct": _safe(pct)}

print("=" * 60)

docs.title: Named Entity Recognition (NER) in Scientific Texts: Custom Rule-Based Pipelines¶
docs.text: Extracting structured domain-specific entities from multi-disciplinary research abstracts using standard spaCy architectures and custom pattern-matching extensions. Built for indexing unstructured literature datasets and metadata cataloging.

docs.title: 1. Setup and Load spaCy¶
docs.text:
This section loads spaCy and the default English model. In an HPC or local environment, install the model first with:

pip install spacy
python -m spacy download en_core_web_sm

docs.title: 2. Sample Scientific Text¶
docs.text: Define a few abstracts that reflect Physics, Genomics, and satellite hydrology research. These examples show the kind of domain text that might be processed in an NMS faculty collaboration.

docs.title: What is Named Entity Recognition?¶
docs.text: Named Entity Recognition (NER) is a task that finds and labels important items in text, like organizations, people, locations, dates, or scientific terms. In a research workflow, NER helps transform unstructured text into structured data that can be searched, filtered, or linked to other datasets.

docs.title: 3. Run the NER Pipeline¶
docs.text: Process each abstract and extract entities along with their labels and explanations. This is the core of a simple NLP extraction pipeline.

docs.title: 4. Visualize Detected Entities¶
docs.text: Use spaCy's displaCy to render entities visually inside the notebook. This can be useful for presenting extraction results to faculty or stakeholders.

docs.title: 5. Add Custom Domain Entities¶
docs.text: Many research domains use terms not recognized by a general English model. An EntityRuler lets us add domain-specific terms such as SWOT, CRISPR-Cas9, and Higgs boson.

docs.title: Semantic Categorization and Zero-Shot Inference Pipelines with Transformer Architectures¶
docs.text: Implementing zero-shot sequence classification and sentiment orientation diagnostics using Hugging Face transformers. This pipeline bypasses supervised training overhead by leveraging cross-lingual natural language inference (NLI) models for instant zero-shot topic distribution and sentiment scoring on scientific texts.

docs.title: What is Sentiment Analysis?¶
docs.text: Sentiment analysis determines whether text expresses a positive, negative, or neutral tone. For research workflows, it can help you screen proposal summaries, feedback, or collaboration updates for overall sentiment.

docs.title: What is Zero-Shot Classification?¶
docs.text: Zero-shot classification lets you assign labels to text without training on those labels first. You provide candidate categories, and the model estimates which category best fits the text.

docs.title: 1. Setup and Load Transformers¶
docs.text: In a fresh environment install transformers and a backend such as PyTorch. Then load the sentiment and zero-shot pipelines.

docs.title: Note on Model Downloads¶
docs.text: HuggingFace pipeline models may need to download weights the first time they run. On HPC systems, make sure you have network access or a pre-cached model directory, and consider running the first cell on a node with internet access before using the notebook offline.

docs.title: 2. Prepare Sample Text¶
docs.text: We use a few research-style sentences that illustrate the kind of faculty-facing text a natural sciences application might process.

docs.title: 3. Run Sentiment Analysis¶
docs.text: This section applies sentiment analysis to each document. It shows how sentiment labels and confidence scores can be extracted from research or proposal text.

docs.title: 4. Run Zero-Shot Classification¶
docs.text: Now we demonstrate zero-shot classification using a set of candidate labels for scientific text. This is useful when you want to categorize new documents into research topics or workflow states without training new models.

docs.title: 5. Notes for Research Applications¶
docs.text:
• Sentiment analysis can be used to screen proposals, feedback, or stakeholder comments.
  • Zero-shot classification is helpful when categories change frequently or when you want to prototype a new taxonomy quickly.
  • For scientific text, you may eventually want to fine-tune on domain-specific labels, but these pipelines provide a strong baseline.
